preamble: The phonetic transformation from the voiceless postalveolar affricate /tʃ/ to the voiceless dental/alveolar affricate /ts/ involves a fronting of the place of articulation. This shift moves the point of constriction from the postalveolar region to the alveolar ridge or teeth. While both sounds are voiceless affricates, the change results in a sharper, more fronted articulation. It is a defining feature of Kashmiri and other Dardic languages, which innovated a dental affricate series from the Old Indo-Aryan palatal affricates.
phoneticEffects: Fronting; change in place of articulation from postalveolar to dental/alveolar

examples.note: 'four' (cf. Sanskrit catvā́raḥ, Hindi cār): the Old Indo-Aryan palatal affricate /tʃ/ developed into the Kashmiri dental affricate /ts/.
'five' (cf. Sanskrit páñca, Hindi pā̃c): inherited /tʃ/ became the dental affricate /ts/.

sources: Grierson, G. A. (1919). Linguistic Survey of India, Vol. VIII, Part II: Specimens of the Dardic or Pisacha Language. Calcutta: Superintendent Government Printing.
Bashir, E. (2003). Dardic. In G. Cardona & D. Jain (Eds.), The Indo-Aryan Languages (pp. 818–894). Routledge.
